title: Waling: the three-rod strength weave
subtitle: Locking transitions and building strength at the base-to-side join
excerpt: Waling uses three rods worked in sequence, each stepping over the next, to produce a tight, strong band that resists deformation. Used at the base-to-side transition of every round basket, at the upsett to lock the stakes into position, and at the border to finish the rim.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. I (1882) for waling descriptions and basket-base construction. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for household basket types and their construction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tight basketry weave in which three rods are worked in sequence, each one stepping over the next. Produces a strong, dense band used at structural points in the basket.
A basketry weave using a single rod woven over and under alternate stakes. The standard filling weave for the main body of a round basket.
The first few rows of waling worked after the stakes are pricked up from the base. The upsett forces the stakes upright and locks them in position before randing begins.
A heavy curved iron bar used to beat down rows of weaving to compact them. The tool that sets the density of the weave in willow basketry.

content.text: If
randing
is the work of the basket,
waling

is the skeleton. Three waling rows at the base lock the stakes upright. Waling rows at the transition from base to side set the angle at which the sides will rise. A final waling band at the top, just below the border, holds the stakes steady while the border is worked. No structural basket is made without it.
When to use waling
Waling is used in three places in a standard round basket. First, the
upsett

: three to four rows of waling worked immediately after the base stakes are pricked up and bent upright. This forces the stakes to stand at the correct angle and holds them in position while randing begins. Second, one or two waling rows midway up the basket wall to lock the randing and prevent the wall from distorting under load. Third, two rows of waling just below the border, to give the border rods something firm to press against as they are folded.
Setting up: inserting three waling rods

Choose three rods of similar size. Insert the butt end of the first rod to the left of any stake, pushing it behind that stake and down into the base. Insert the second rod similarly, one stake to the right of the first. Insert the third rod one stake further right. All three butts are now behind adjacent stakes with their working ends extending to the right.
Working the waling sequence
The waling step

Take the leftmost rod. Pass it in front of the next two stakes to the right, then behind the third stake, then out to the front again. Leave it there. Take the new leftmost rod (which was the second rod) and repeat: in front of two stakes, behind one, out. Continue with the third rod in the same way. This sequence of take-the-leftmost, in-front-of-two, behind-one is the waling step and it repeats continuously.
After each waling step, use the
rapping iron

to beat down the newly worked rod against the row below. The rapping iron is held flat against the weave and struck or pressed firmly downward. This packs the rods tightly and is what gives waling its density. Do not skip this step; loose waling does not hold the stakes firmly.
The upsett

At the upsett stage, the basket is held upside down in the lap or between the knees, base facing up. The three waling rods are inserted and the first rows worked as described, with firm downward pressure at each step and consistent rapping. Three to four rows of upsett waling are standard. As the rows accumulate, the stakes will stiffen and begin to hold their angle. After the upsett, turn the basket right-side up to continue with randing.
Joining new waling rods

When a waling rod runs short, join it butt-to-tip: bring the tip of the old rod behind the current stake and lay the butt of the new rod alongside it, both behind the same stake. Continue with the new rod. Join each of the three rods at different points around the basket; joining all three at the same position produces a weak point and a visible vertical line.
What good waling looks like

The waling band should look dense and even, like a tight braid. The rods sit flush against one another with no gaps visible between them. When pressed with a fingertip, there is no give. The stakes it covers stand at a consistent angle with no wobble. If the waling has a loose, uneven look, either the rapping was insufficient or the rods were too thin for the stake spacing.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that come up in waling.

items.symptom: The three rods get twisted and tangled as the waling progresses
items.cause: The rods are not being taken in the correct left-to-right order. If two rods are taken out of sequence, they cross and tangle.
items.fix: Stop and identify which rod is leftmost at each step. Mark the rod order with rubber bands if needed until the sequence is established.

items.symptom: The waling rows are loose even after rapping
items.cause: The willow rods are too thin for the stake gauge, or the soaking was insufficient and the rods are still stiff.
items.fix: Use thicker or better-soaked rods for the waling. The waling rods should be at least as thick as the stakes, ideally slightly thicker.

items.symptom: The stakes lean outward or inward after the upsett
items.cause: The upsett angle was not set correctly. If the stakes leaned outward before the first waling row was rapped, the upsett locks them in that position.
items.fix: Before beginning the upsett, hold the stakes at the correct angle with a hoop or template ring and complete the first row before releasing. The first rapped row fixes the angle for subsequent rows.
